threats.title: VoteWeighting.removeNominee Corrupts Global pointsSum Slope, Distorting All Emission Distributions
threats.hypothesis_description: When owner calls removeNominee(), the function zeros pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].bias and recalculates pointsSum[nextTime].bias as oldSum - oldWeight. However, it does NOT remove the nominee's slope contribution from pointsSum[nextTime].slope. The removed nominee's slope remains in the global sum, causing pointsSum.bias to decay faster than the actual sum of active nominee weights. This progressively distorts all nomineeRelativeWeight calculations and disrupts the entire staking incentive distribution system.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a logic error in a critical governance operation. The admin will inevitably call removeNominee during normal protocol operations. Each removal permanently corrupts the global weight sum. Over time, the accumulated slope errors compound, making relative weights increasingly inaccurate for all active nominees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pointsSum[nextTime].bias is updated to newSum but pointsSum[nextTime].slope is NOT adjusted to remove the removed nominee's slope contribution

preconditions: Owner calls removeNominee for a nominee that has active vote weight
The removed nominee had non-zero slope in the global sum
Other nominees remain active

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: pointsWeight bias zeroed, pointsSum bias updated, but pointsSum slope not adjusted
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After: pointsSum[nextTime].bias = newSum (correct), but pointsSum[nextTime].slope still includes removed nominee's slope (wrong). Future _getSum() calls will over-decay the sum bias.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No slope adjustment for pointsSum when removing nominee; no cleanup of changesSum entries related to removed nominee's voters

analysis_questions: How quickly does the slope error compound across weeks?
Can the corrupted sum become less than an individual nominee's weight, causing >100% relative weight?
Does this lead to excess or deficient staking incentive distribution?
validation_requirements: Simulate removeNominee with active votes and trace pointsSum evolution over subsequent weeks
Verify that nomineeRelativeWeight returns values > 1e18 after sufficient time
Calculate total staking incentives distributed vs. expected under correct accounting

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner removes deprecated nominee. Global sum slope retains removed nominee's slope, causing sum bias to decrease faster than actual nominee weights. All remaining nominees get distorted relative weights. Staking incentive calculations produce incorrect allocations across all chains.
prerequisites: At least one nominee removal with non-trivial voting weight

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Systematic misallocation of OLAS staking incentives across all nominees and chains. Could lead to underdistribution or over-allocation depending on the direction of the distortion.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All staking targets and their stakers across all chains
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or redeployment of VoteWeighting

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for post-removal pointsSum slope consistency

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: N/A - triggered by normal admin operation
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Triggered on each removeNominee call; compounds over weeks
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing on-chain sum of individual nominee weights vs pointsSum.bias

threats.title: L2 Target Dispenser Batch Processing DoS via Malicious Staking Contract deposit() Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: In DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData(), the verification of staking targets uses a low-level call (which handles failures gracefully), but the subsequent IStaking(target).deposit(amount) is a regular Solidity call. If a valid-looking staking target's deposit() function reverts, the entire _processData transaction reverts, blocking ALL other targets in the same bridge message batch from receiving their OLAS incentives.
threats.investigation_rationale: The StakingFactory's permissionless createStakingInstance allows anyone to deploy staking instances. A malicious instance could pass all verifier checks but have a deposit() that conditionally reverts. Since _processData processes all targets in a single transaction without try/catch around deposit(), one bad target blocks the entire batch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Low-level call for verification (line 147-148) vs regular call for deposit (line 165); deposit revert propagates to entire function

preconditions: Attacker deploys a staking instance via StakingFactory that passes verifier checks
Attacker gets the instance voted for in VoteWeighting (requires veOLAS)
The instance's deposit() function conditionally reverts
Bridge message batch includes both the malicious and legitimate targets

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: IToken(olas).approve(target, amount); IStaking(target).deposit(amount); — no try/catch, revert propagates
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A single reverting deposit() blocks all targets in the batch. Factory verification at lines 147-153 uses low-level call but deposit at line 165 does not.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try/catch around deposit(), no per-target error handling

analysis_questions: Can a staking contract pass all verifier checks while having a conditionally reverting deposit()?
How often are multiple targets batched in a single bridge message?
What is the recovery path via processDataMaintenance?
validation_requirements: Create a staking contract that passes StakingVerifier checks but reverts on deposit()
Demonstrate batch DoS by including it in a multi-target bridge message
Verify that legitimate targets are permanently blocked until DAO intervention

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys malicious staking instance through factory, gets votes directed to it, bridge message batches it with legitimate targets, deposit() reverts blocking entire batch, requires DAO to call processDataMaintenance excluding malicious target.
prerequisites: veOLAS holding for voting
Ability to deploy staking instance via factory

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary denial of staking incentives for all L2 targets on the affected chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All staking programs on the affected L2 chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Recoverable via DAO processDataMaintenance call, but delayed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for deposit() revert handling in batch processing

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Triggered when bridge message is delivered to L2
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Bridge message delivery failure visible on-chain

threats.title: DefaultTargetDispenserL2.redeem Bypasses Staking Target Re-verification
threats.hypothesis_description: The redeem() function in DefaultTargetDispenserL2 allows anyone to process queued staking deposits by providing the target, amount, and batchNonce that match a stored queue hash. However, redeem() does NOT re-verify the staking target against StakingFactory. A target that was valid when queued but later disabled (via setInstanceStatus) or exceeded its emissions limit can still receive the full queued OLAS amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: Queued deposits represent deferred processing of OLAS incentives. The time gap between queueing (_processData) and redemption (redeem) can be significant. During this window, the staking target's validity can change. The absence of re-verification means the emissions limit check that _processData performed is not enforced at redemption time.

proof_of_signal.pattern: redeem() checks only stakingQueueingNonces[queueHash] existence; no call to stakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount

preconditions: A staking deposit was queued during _processData (insufficient OLAS balance or paused state)
The target staking instance was subsequently disabled or its emissions limit reduced
Someone calls redeem with the valid queue hash

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Only queueHash existence check at line 251; approve and deposit at lines 258-259 without verifying target validity
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: IStaking(target).deposit(amount) executes without checking if target is still a valid staking instance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No call to verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount in redeem path

analysis_questions: How frequently do targets get disabled between queueing and redemption?
Can the amount exceed the updated emissions limit?
Is there any other mechanism preventing deposits to disabled instances?
validation_requirements: Queue a deposit, disable the instance, then call redeem to verify OLAS is deposited to disabled instance
Verify that the deposit succeeds despite the instance being disabled in the factory

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Deposit queued for legitimate amount during temporary balance shortage. Target instance later disabled by deployer or fails re-verification. Anyone calls redeem() and OLAS is deposited to the now-invalid target, bypassing emissions limits.
prerequisites: Queued deposit exists
Target validity changes between queue and redeem

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: OLAS deposited to invalid or over-limit staking targets, potentially lost or misallocated
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol treasury and legitimate staking participants
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: OLAS sent to invalid target may be irrecoverable

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for redeem after target status change

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: StakingTargetDeposited event to disabled instance

threats.title: Tokenomics.refundFromStaking Uses Wrong Address in Error Message (depository instead of dispenser)
threats.hypothesis_description: In Tokenomics.refundFromStaking(), the access control check correctly validates msg.sender against the dispenser address, but the error message erroneously references the depository address: `revert ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository)`. This is a definite code defect where the error reports the wrong required address.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a provably incorrect error parameter. The function is gated by dispenser access but the revert message says depository. While not directly exploitable for fund theft, it's a code correctness bug that could mislead debugging, monitoring tools, and incident response during security events.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Access check is `dispenser != msg.sender` but error is `revert ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository)` — should be dispenser

preconditions: Any non-dispenser address calls refundFromStaking

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if (dispenser != msg.sender) { revert ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository); }
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Error message parameter is `depository` but should be `dispenser`
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: N/A — code defect in error reporting

analysis_questions: Are there other similar copy-paste error message bugs in access control checks?
Could the misleading error cause monitoring tools to miss actual security events?
validation_requirements: Call refundFromStaking from a non-dispenser address and verify error contains depository address instead of dispenser

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: No direct attack scenario. This is a code correctness bug. The access control itself works correctly — only the error message is wrong.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but misleading error in security monitoring
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Developers and monitoring systems
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Fix in next deployment

threats.test_coverage_gap: Error message content not validated in tests

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible in any failed call to refundFromStaking

threats.title: EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit Hard Reverts on Any Invalid Target, Blocking Entire Batch
threats.hypothesis_description: In EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit(), if any single target in a batch returns zero from verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount, the function reverts with TargetEmissionsZero, blocking ALL targets in the batch. This differs from L2 dispensers which gracefully withhold invalid amounts and continue processing. On L1, a single disabled staking instance blocks the entire chain's incentive distribution.
threats.investigation_rationale: The L1 EthereumDepositProcessor lacks the graceful error handling present in L2 DefaultTargetDispenserL2. A staking instance deployer can disable their instance via setInstanceStatus(false) between the time calculateStakingIncentives runs and when sendMessage/sendMessageBatch delivers the deposit. This creates a DoS window.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (limitAmount == 0) { revert TargetEmissionsZero(target); } — hard revert blocks entire batch

preconditions: Multiple staking targets exist on L1 (Ethereum chain)
One target becomes invalid (disabled by deployer or fails verification) between calculation and deposit
Batch claim includes the invalid target

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Hard revert on zero emissions for any target in batch, unlike L2 which withholds gracefully
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Inconsistent error handling between L1 and L2 deposit processors; L1 has no fallback for invalid targets in batch
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try/catch or graceful withholding mechanism like L2 _processData

analysis_questions: Can a deployer time instance disabling to consistently block batch processing?
What is the recovery path when a batch is blocked?
How many targets are typically batched together on L1?
validation_requirements: Disable a target between calculateStakingIncentives and sendMessageBatch calls
Verify that the entire batch reverts including valid targets
Test single vs batch claiming to understand the blast radius

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys staking instance, gets votes, then disables it. When anyone claims the L1 batch, the entire batch reverts because one target has zero emissions. All valid L1 staking targets are blocked from receiving incentives.
prerequisites: Attacker has deployed and gotten votes for a staking instance on L1
Batch claiming is used

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary DoS blocking all L1 staking incentive distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L1 staking targets in the same batch
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Individual claims per target would work, but batch efficiency is lost

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for batch failure on partial target invalidity

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Revert on claimStakingIncentivesBatch

threats.title: Tokenomics._finalizeIncentivesForUnitId Silent uint96 Truncation on Reward and TopUp Accumulation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _finalizeIncentivesForUnitId(), the accumulated reward and topUp values are computed in uint256 but cast to uint96 without overflow checks: `mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].reward = uint96(totalIncentives)` and `mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].topUp = uint96(totalIncentives)`. If totalIncentives exceeds type(uint96).max (~7.9e28), the value silently truncates, permanently destroying accumulated rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: While uint96 provides ~79 billion tokens with 18 decimals, the accumulation formula multiplies pendingRelativeTopUp * totalTopUpsOLAS * topUpUnitFraction, which could produce values exceeding uint96 for popular components/agents with large inflation schedules. The cast has no revert check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint96(totalIncentives) cast without overflow check; totalIncentives computed from multi-factor multiplication

preconditions: A component/agent accumulates large pending rewards or top-ups across multiple epochs
The multiplication of pendingRelativeTopUp * totalTopUpsOLAS * topUpUnitFraction exceeds uint96 before division
Owner does not claim frequently enough

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].reward = uint96(totalIncentives); — no overflow check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The intermediate totalIncentives is uint256 but the storage cast to uint96 silently truncates values > ~7.9e28
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(totalIncentives <= type(uint96).max) or SafeCast

analysis_questions: What is the maximum realistic totalIncentives value given current inflation parameters?
How many epochs of unclaimed rewards would trigger overflow?
Does the topUp calculation path have a higher overflow risk than the reward path?
validation_requirements: Calculate maximum possible totalIncentives for both reward and topUp paths
Simulate multi-epoch accumulation without claiming to find overflow threshold
Verify silent truncation behavior at boundary values

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A highly-utilized component accumulates rewards across many epochs. When finalization occurs, the uint96 cast truncates the accumulated value. The component owner permanently loses the truncated portion of their rewards.
prerequisites: Significant epoch-over-epoch accumulation without claiming
Large donation volumes or inflation amounts

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of accumulated rewards/top-ups for affected unit owners
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Component/agent owners with large unclaimed balances
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lost funds cannot be recovered — truncation is irreversible

threats.test_coverage_gap: No overflow boundary testing for uint96 casts

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None for attacker, but requires large protocol activity
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing expected vs actual reward values post-finalization

threats.title: VoteWeighting Removed Nominee's changesSum Entries Persist and Distort Future Slope Adjustments
threats.hypothesis_description: When removeNominee is called, it does not clean up the changesWeight and changesSum entries scheduled at future lock expiration times of users who voted for the removed nominee. If users do not voluntarily call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower, these scheduled slope decrements will fire at the original lock expiration times, incorrectly reducing the global sum's slope and further distorting weight calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users have no obligation or incentive to revoke votes from removed nominees. The protocol relies on user cooperation for cleanup, but game theory suggests many users will not bother. Each unrevoked vote creates a scheduled future corruption point in changesSum.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeNominee does not iterate over voters to clean changesSum/changesWeight at their lock expiration times

preconditions: Nominee is removed while multiple users have active votes directed to it
Users do not call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower
Lock expirations of those users arrive in the future

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No cleanup of changesSum entries at voter lock expiration times during nominee removal
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: changesSum[lockEnd] will still decrement slope at lock expiration even though the nominee was removed, causing double-counting of slope reduction
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Protocol relies on voluntary user action (revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower) for cleanup

analysis_questions: What percentage of users are likely to call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower?
How significant is the slope distortion from unrevoked votes?
Can this compound with TECH-H001 to accelerate global sum corruption?
validation_requirements: Remove nominee with 5+ voters, wait for lock expirations without revocation
Measure pointsSum slope errors after lock expirations fire
Compare nomineeRelativeWeight accuracy before and after

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Popular nominee removed by admin. Most voters don't revoke. When their veOLAS locks expire, changesSum incorrectly decrements the global slope for weight that was already removed. This causes pointsSum to undercount, further distorting relative weights for all remaining nominees.
prerequisites: Nominee removal with multiple active voters
Voters not revoking power

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Progressive distortion of staking incentive distribution across all nominees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All active staking nominees and their stakers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partially recoverable if all users revoke, but practically unlikely

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for changesSum consistency after nominee removal without user revocation

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Fires at user lock expiration times
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring pointsSum.slope vs sum of individual nominee slopes

threats.title: Dispenser.claimOwnerIncentives Reverts When Reward and TopUp Are Both Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: In claimOwnerIncentives(), when both reward and topUp are zero, the code path skips the Treasury withdrawal (since reward + topUp == 0), leaving the `success` variable as its default value `false`. The function then hits `if (!success) { revert ClaimIncentivesFailed(...); }`, causing an uninformative revert instead of a graceful return with zero values.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code logic defect. Any user calling claimOwnerIncentives when they have no accumulated incentives will receive a confusing ClaimIncentivesFailed error instead of a clean return. The `success` variable is only set inside the `if ((reward + topUp) > 0)` block, so when both are zero it remains false.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bool success; (default false) set inside conditional block; reverts when success is false even for legitimate zero-incentive case

preconditions: User calls claimOwnerIncentives with valid unitTypes and unitIds
The unit has no accumulated reward or topUp

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: success remains false when reward+topUp==0; line 460: if (!success) revert
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Legitimate call with zero incentives gets reverted with misleading error instead of returning (0, 0)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No special handling for zero-incentive case

analysis_questions: Can this cause issues for integration contracts that try-catch claim calls?
Does this affect off-chain tooling that checks for claim availability?
validation_requirements: Call claimOwnerIncentives for a unit with no incentives and verify the revert
Confirm the revert message is ClaimIncentivesFailed with zero values

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not directly exploitable for fund extraction. This is a code correctness issue causing confusing reverts for users with no accumulated incentives.
prerequisites: User calls claim with zero accumulated incentives

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct financial impact, but breaks integrations expecting graceful zero returns
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users and integration contracts calling claimOwnerIncentives
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: N/A — user can check getOwnerIncentives first

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for zero-incentive claim path

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible in any zero-incentive claim attempt

threats.title: StakingFactory Owner Cannot Disable Malicious Staking Instances Created by Untrusted Deployers
threats.hypothesis_description: StakingFactory.setInstanceStatus() restricts instance enable/disable to the original deployer (msg.sender == instanceParams.deployer). The factory owner has NO override capability. If a deployer creates a staking instance that passes initial verification but later behaves maliciously, the factory owner cannot disable it. Since createStakingInstance is permissionless, any attacker can deploy instances that only they can control.
threats.investigation_rationale: The factory is permissionless and anyone can deploy instances. The verifier checks are limited to parameter bounds (rewardsPerSecond, maxNumServices, stakingToken). A malicious implementation could pass these checks but contain harmful logic. The factory owner cannot intervene to disable such instances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != deployer) revert OwnerOnly — only deployer can toggle; factory owner has no authority

preconditions: Attacker deploys staking instance via factory (permissionless)
Instance passes initial verification
Instance later exhibits malicious behavior
Factory owner needs to disable it

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Only deployer check, no factory owner override
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Factory owner (likely DAO) cannot emergency-disable malicious instances
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No owner override in setInstanceStatus

analysis_questions: Can the factory verifier be updated to blacklist specific instances?
Does the verifier's verifyInstance provide an alternative disable mechanism?
What damage can a malicious but verified instance cause?
validation_requirements: Verify that factory owner cannot call setInstanceStatus for any instance
Test if changing verifier can effectively disable specific instances

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys malicious staking instance, gets votes directed to it. OLAS emissions flow to it. Factory owner cannot disable it. The verifier can be updated but this is a blunt instrument affecting all instances.
prerequisites: Attacker deploys instance through permissionless factory

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Continued OLAS emissions to malicious staking contracts
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol treasury and staking participants
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial — verifier update can mitigate, but specific instance targeting requires implementation whitelist changes

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for factory owner attempting to disable instances

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via factory and verifier state

threats.title: DefaultTargetDispenserL2 Leaves Dangling ERC20 Approval After Failed deposit() During _processData
threats.hypothesis_description: In _processData(), when a staking target passes verification and the contract has sufficient OLAS balance, it calls IToken(olas).approve(target, amount) followed by IStaking(target).deposit(amount). If deposit() reverts, the entire transaction reverts (no try/catch), which safely cleans up the approval. HOWEVER, if the target's deposit() function partially consumes the approval (transfers less than approved amount), a dangling approval remains. Additionally, during redeem(), approve+deposit pattern is repeated without revoking prior approvals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The approve-then-deposit pattern without approval cleanup could leave residual allowances. While standard staking contracts consume exact amounts, a malicious target could intentionally underspend the approval and later drain the remaining allowance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: approve(target, amount) followed by deposit(amount) without approval revocation on any code path

preconditions: Staking target's deposit() function does not consume the full approved amount
Target contract has ability to call transferFrom on the dispenser's OLAS
Target passes factory verification

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: approve(target, amount) without subsequent approval reset to 0
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If deposit() consumes less than approved, target retains allowance to drain more OLAS later
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No approve(target, 0) after deposit, no balance check to verify exact consumption

analysis_questions: Can a staking contract's deposit() consume less than the approved amount?
Does the StakingToken.deposit() always consume the exact amount via safeTransferFrom?
Is there any code path where approval is not fully consumed?
validation_requirements: Deploy a staking contract whose deposit() transfers less than approved
Verify that residual approval exists after _processData
Demonstrate that the target can later drain OLAS via the residual approval

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Malicious staking contract passes verification, receives approval during _processData, deposits only partial amount, retains residual allowance. Later calls transferFrom on the dispenser to steal the remaining approved OLAS.
prerequisites: Malicious staking contract that passes verification
deposit() intentionally underspends approval

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: OLAS theft from L2 dispenser up to the approved amount
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All staking participants on the affected L2 chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lost OLAS irrecoverable from malicious target

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for partial approval consumption by staking targets

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: veOLAS for voting weight to direct emissions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring OLAS allowances from dispenser to targets

threats.title: Donation Amount Truncation in _trackServiceDonations Creates Undistributed ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: In _trackServiceDonations(), each donation amount is divided by the number of service units: `uint96 amount = uint96(amounts[i] / numServiceUnits)`. The remainder is lost — it is counted in totalDonationsETH but never distributed to any unit's pendingRelativeReward or pendingRelativeTopUp. Over many donations with remainders, this creates a permanent discrepancy between recorded donations and actual distributed rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The totalDonationsETH includes the full donation amount, but the per-unit distribution loses the remainder. Treasury rewards are calculated from totalDonationsETH * rewardTreasuryFraction / 100, while unit rewards use the truncated amounts. This means the treasury fraction is correctly calculated on the full amount, but unit rewards are calculated on truncated amounts, leaving some reward value permanently unallocated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint96 amount = uint96(amounts[i] / numServiceUnits); — integer division truncation, remainder lost

preconditions: Services have multiple units (agents/components)
Donation amounts are not perfectly divisible by number of units
Cumulative truncation over many donations becomes non-trivial

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Integer division truncation: amounts[i] / numServiceUnits loses remainder
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Truncated remainder contributes to totalDonationsETH (line 399) but is never distributed to any unit, creating permanent accounting imbalance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No accumulator for truncation remainders

analysis_questions: What is the expected cumulative truncation loss over realistic usage?
Does this lead to permanently locked ETH in the treasury?
Can services with many units be used to amplify the truncation?
validation_requirements: Calculate truncation loss for various service configurations
Verify that totalDonationsETH - sum(distributed rewards) > 0 after donations
Test with services having maximum number of units

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not directly exploitable for profit, but creates a systematic leak where recorded donations exceed distributed rewards. Services with many units amplify the effect. Over time, this could create noticeable discrepancies between expected and actual reward distributions.
prerequisites: Multi-unit services receiving non-round donation amounts

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Minor systematic loss per donation, potentially significant at scale
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Component/agent owners receiving slightly less than their proportional share
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Undistributed amounts remain in treasury

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for donation truncation accounting

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing totalDonationsETH vs sum of unit distributions

threats.title: Permissionless Tokenomics initialization enables permanent takeover if deployed/used as standalone implementation
threats.hypothesis_description: Tokenomics.initializeTokenomics() is external and only guarded by `owner == address(0)`. If the Tokenomics implementation contract is deployed and left uninitialized (or is intended to be used standalone), any attacker can initialize it first, becoming `owner` and permanently controlling managers (treasury/depository/dispenser), registries, parameters, and the PROXY_TOKENOMICS implementation slot used as a sentinel for checkpoint().
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a classic, highly realistic takeover path when an implementation is deployed without an atomic initialization step or if the protocol uses the contract directly rather than through a pre-initialized proxy. Impact is complete control over tokenomics configuration and incentive flows.
deployment_assumptions: Tokenomics.sol is deployed as a standalone contract OR a proxy delegates to it without an atomic initializeTokenomics call
No deployment script/constructor guarantees initialization in the same transaction
threats.config_evidence: No deployment scripts provided; initializeTokenomics is external and unrestricted beyond owner==0 guard.

proof_of_signal.lines: initializeTokenomics: owner is set to msg.sender; only guard is `if (owner != address(0)) revert AlreadyInitialized();`
proof_of_signal.pattern: external initializer without access control beyond 'only once' check

preconditions: Tokenomics contract storage `owner` is still zero
Attacker can send a transaction to initializeTokenomics
Contract is not already initialized atomically at deployment

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless first-caller initialization
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: attacker becomes Tokenomics.owner and can rewire treasury/dispenser/depository and tokenomics parameters
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no onlyProxy / onlyDeployer restriction; no constructor disabling initialization

analysis_questions: Is Tokenomics intended to be used only behind a pre-initialized proxy, or can it be deployed standalone?
Do deployment scripts ensure initializeTokenomics is called atomically?
Are there any external guardrails in Treasury/Dispenser that would prevent damage if Tokenomics.owner is attacker?
validation_requirements: Review deployment procedure (scripts/ops runbooks) for atomic initialization
Check whether Tokenomics is ever interacted with directly (not via proxy)
Attempt to initialize a fresh Tokenomics instance in tests and confirm full admin control post-init

threats.title: Permissionless staking implementation initialization allows attacker-controlled staking instances with arbitrary parameters
threats.hypothesis_description: StakingToken.initialize() and StakingNativeToken.initialize() are external and have no access control beyond StakingBase._initialize single-init check (serviceRegistry != address(0)). Any actor can deploy these implementations standalone (or any proxy pointing to them) and initialize with arbitrary parameters (serviceRegistry, activityChecker, rewardsPerSecond, proxyHash, etc.). This can create malicious staking targets that appear valid to downstream systems depending on verification configuration, potentially siphoning emissions (if voted for) or griefing distribution flows.
threats.investigation_rationale: The system explicitly allows permissionless staking contract creation via factory, but standalone deployments plus weak/optional verifier settings can expand the attack surface. Realistic if verifier is unset or misconfigured, or if consumers do not strictly require factory verification on all chains.
deployment_assumptions: StakingVerifier / StakingFactory verification is optional or not enforced on a target chain (verifier == 0 or implementationsCheck == false)
VoteWeighting allows nominee addition for arbitrary staking targets (as designed)
threats.config_evidence: StakingFactory constructor allows verifier to be zero; StakingVerifier implementationsCheck can be false; no deployment evidence provided.

proof_of_signal.lines: initialize(...) is external; calls _initialize(...) then sets stakingToken/serviceRegistryTokenUtility with no caller restriction
proof_of_signal.pattern: external initializer without ownership/role guard (only single-init)

preconditions: Attacker can deploy a staking instance (standalone or via any proxy) pointing to these implementations
Downstream emissions routing (L2 dispensers / L1 processor) accepts targets based on weak verification settings or social layer listing

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: attacker can craft staking contract behavior/params to maximize emissions or create denial-of-service via invalid configs
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no onlyFactory / onlyProxy / onlyDeployer guard

End-to-end test: VoteWeighting nominee -> Dispenser claim -> L2 deposit -> staking target receives OLAS

Sufficient votes directed to attacker nominee

threats.title: Dispenser claimStakingIncentives can transfer/mint OLAS to a zero or malicious depositProcessor if chainId mapping is unset or set to non-contract
threats.hypothesis_description: claimStakingIncentives / claimStakingIncentivesBatch use mapChainIdDepositProcessors[chainId] without validating it is non-zero and a contract before calling getBridgingDecimals() and transferring OLAS to it. If the mapping is unset (0) or accidentally set to an EOA, calls will revert (DoS) or OLAS may be transferred to an address that cannot forward it across the bridge (funds stuck).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical failure mode that attackers can trigger by choosing specific chainIds/targets already registered in VoteWeighting (or if a chainId nominee exists). Since anyone can call claim functions, they can repeatedly hit a chainId with bad configuration to cause consistent DoS. Also, if treasury minting succeeds, tokens can be stranded if transfers occur before failing external message calls in some paths (batch ordering).
deployment_assumptions: At least one chainId used by nominees has mapChainIdDepositProcessors[chainId] == address(0) OR points to non-contract
Users can call claimStakingIncentives for that chainId
threats.config_evidence: Dispenser.setDepositProcessorChainIds allows setting depositProcessors[i] to zero explicitly ('might be zero if there is a need to stop processing').

proof_of_signal.lines: setDepositProcessorChainIds: 'depositProcessors[i] might be zero'; claimStakingIncentives uses depositProcessor = mapChainIdDepositProcessors[chainId] then calls IDepositProcessor(depositProcessor).getBridgingDecimals()
proof_of_signal.pattern: unvalidated external call to potentially-zero / non-contract address

preconditions: A nominee exists for a chainId where deposit processor is unset/EOA
Caller invokes claimStakingIncentives or claimStakingIncentivesBatch with that chainId

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: allows zero address configuration; later assumes contract interface
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: DoS of claiming or stuck tokens if transferred to wrong address
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no require(depositProcessor.code.length>0) in claim/distribution paths

analysis_questions: Is it intended that setting depositProcessor to zero disables claims, and is that acceptable?
Do claim functions ever transfer OLAS before a call that could revert (batch scenarios) such that partial state is impacted?
Are there chainIds in VoteWeighting that could be claimed while depositProcessor is unset?
validation_requirements: Review operational configuration for mapChainIdDepositProcessors across supported chainIds
Simulate claim paths with depositProcessor=0 and with depositProcessor=EOA
Confirm whether tokens could be stranded in Dispenser in any partial-success scenario

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly calls claimStakingIncentives for a chainId whose depositProcessor is unset/EOA, forcing consistent revert/DoS for automated claiming bots; alternatively, if an EOA is set mistakenly, minted OLAS can be transferred to that EOA and become unrecoverable.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration or deliberate disabling of a chainId deposit processor

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Stuck emissions or repeated claim failures; potential stranded OLAS if transferred to unusable address
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All staking targets on affected chainId
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Fixable by updating mapping, but stranded tokens may be unrecoverable if sent to wrong address

threats.title: StakingBase Checkpoint Reward Rounding Systematically Favors First Eligible Service in Array
threats.hypothesis_description: When totalRewards exceeds lastAvailableRewards in the checkpoint function, rewards are proportionally scaled down. The loop processes services from index 1 first, then handles service[0] last, assigning any rounding dust (lastAvailableRewards - updatedTotalRewards) to it. The first eligible service consistently receives extra wei from rounding. While individually tiny, this is a systematic code defect in reward distribution fairness.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly processes service[0] separately and adds any leftover dust to it. This creates a deterministic advantage for whichever service occupies position 0 in the eligibleServiceIds array. Over many checkpoints, the dust accumulates for the same service.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop starts from i=1, processes service[0] last with dust addition: if (lastAvailableRewards > updatedTotalRewards) { updatedReward += lastAvailableRewards - updatedTotalRewards; }

preconditions: totalRewards > lastAvailableRewards (proportional scaling path)
Multiple eligible services exist
Rounding creates non-zero dust (common with integer division)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: updatedReward = (eligibleServiceRewards[i] * lastAvailableRewards) / totalRewards creates rounding dust
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First service in array always receives the rounding residual
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Dust per checkpoint is small (1-N wei where N = numServices), but accumulates over many checkpoints

economic_questions: What is the maximum dust amount per checkpoint?
How is the order of eligibleServiceIds determined?
Can a service manipulate its position in the array?
validation_requirements: Calculate typical dust amounts for realistic scenarios
Verify eligibleServiceIds ordering matches setServiceIds ordering
Assess cumulative impact over hundreds of checkpoints

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of deploying and staking a service

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate proportional scaling with realistic values

threats.title: Wormhole Bridge Precision Loss Causes Permanent L2 Staking Queue Lockup
threats.hypothesis_description: Wormhole's TokenBridge truncates ERC20 transfers to 8 decimals. When Dispenser processes a claim with a withheldAmount deduction, the transferAmount can have up to 18 decimals of precision. The WormholeDepositProcessorL1 sends this amount via Wormhole, which silently truncates the dust. However, the cross-chain message payload encodes the ORIGINAL stakingIncentives array. On L2, WormholeTargetDispenserL2 attempts to distribute the full stakingIncentives amount, but its actual OLAS balance is short by the truncated dust. This causes the balance check to fail, pushing the target into the queue. Because the L2 contract is permanently short of funds, these queued incentives can NEVER be redeemed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The mismatch between the bridged token amount (truncated to 8 decimals) and the message payload amount (18 decimals) creates a permanent insolvency on the L2 dispenser, locking user rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sendTokenWithPayloadToEvm(..., transferAmount, ...) truncates to 8 decimals while payload contains 18-decimal amounts

preconditions: Wormhole bridge is used for L2 target
A previous claim resulted in a withheldAmount with dust (not a multiple of 1e10)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: transferAmount -= withheldAmount creates dust; Wormhole truncates dust; L2 expects full amount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any dust in withheldAmount causes all subsequent batches to fail balance check on L2
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but permanent lockup of all staking incentives for the affected chain

economic_questions: Can withheldAmount realistically contain dust?
Does Wormhole TokenBridge strictly truncate to 8 decimals for OLAS?
validation_requirements: Verify Wormhole TokenBridge decimal handling
Test L2 balance check failure when payload amount > received amount

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: None (can occur naturally or via 1 wei manipulation)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after a withheldAmount with dust is generated
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete halt of staking incentive distribution on Wormhole-connected L2s

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All staking service operators on the affected L2
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All future OLAS emissions directed to the affected L2

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Wormhole bridging decimals (8) vs OLAS decimals (18)

threats.title: Array Out-of-Bounds DoS in unstake Due to Stale Index After Eviction
threats.hypothesis_description: In StakingBase.sol, unstake calls checkpoint(), which may evict inactive services. The _evict function removes services by swapping them with the last element in setServiceIds and popping. However, unstake determines the index (idx) of the unstaking service from a COPY of the array taken BEFORE evictions occur. If another service is evicted during the checkpoint, and the unstaking service happens to be at the end of the array, _evict will move the unstaking service and reduce the array length. When unstake attempts to remove the service using its stale idx, it accesses an out-of-bounds index, causing the transaction to revert and permanently locking the user's service NFT and rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using an index derived from a pre-mutation array copy to mutate the post-mutation array leads to out-of-bounds access or incorrect element removal.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setServiceIds[idx] = setServiceIds[setServiceIds.length - 1]; uses stale idx from before checkpoint()

preconditions: User calls unstake on a service located at the end of setServiceIds
Another service in the array is evicted during the internal checkpoint() call

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker can intentionally trigger evictions to DoS other users' unstake transactions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack; locks victim's NFT and accumulated rewards

economic_questions: Can an attacker reliably trigger this to lock specific high-value services?
validation_requirements: Simulate unstake of the last array element while another element is eligible for eviction

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (cost to stake a dummy service and let it become inactive)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user funds and NFTs

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of staked NFT and pending rewards
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Value of staked NFTs and accumulated rewards

threats.title: Dispenser.retain() Callable During Staking Incentive Pause, Advancing Retainer Epoch Counter and Modifying Staking Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: The retain() function in Dispenser.sol lacks any pause state check, unlike claimStakingIncentives and claimStakingIncentivesBatch which both verify paused state and treasury paused state. This allows anyone to call retain() during a StakingIncentivesPaused or AllPaused state, advancing mapLastClaimedStakingEpochs[retainerHash] and calling ITokenomics.refundFromStaking() which modifies the current epoch's staking incentive accounting. During a security pause meant to freeze all staking-related operations, retain() remains operational and can alter staking inflation state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pause mechanism is specifically designed to halt staking incentive flows during emergencies. retain() is functionally part of the staking incentive system (it returns unused incentives to inflation), so its exclusion from pause checks appears to be an oversight.

proof_of_signal.pattern: retain() has reentrancy guard but NO pause check; contrast with claimStakingIncentives lines 490-493 which check paused == StakingIncentivesPaused || paused == AllPaused || ITreasury(treasury).paused() == 2

preconditions: Dispenser is in StakingIncentivesPaused or AllPaused state
Retainer has unclaimed epochs
Retainer has non-zero voting weight

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: retain() operates on staking incentive data without pause protection
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Anyone can call retain() at any time regardless of pause state
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any staking incentive pause period

operational_questions: Does calling retain() during pause cause any state inconsistency with post-unpause operations?
Can refundFromStaking during pause affect epoch settlement calculations?
Is the advancing of retainerHash epoch counter recoverable?
validation_requirements: Set pause state to StakingIncentivesPaused, call retain(), verify it succeeds
Verify claimStakingIncentives reverts in the same pause state
Check if refundFromStaking changes affect checkpoint() calculations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for owner to pause staking incentives (e.g., during security incident)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call retain() to advance retainer's claimed epochs and refund to inflation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: This modifies staking incentive accounting during what should be a frozen state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: After unpause, the staking incentive distribution reflects the mid-pause retain() effects

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Pause protection for staking incentive accounting is incomplete
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Staking inflation state modified during emergency pause
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - effects are limited to inflation pool refunds

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Staking inflation changes during emergency pause
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Opportunistic retain() calls timed with pause events

threats.title: Tokenomics.changeTokenomicsParameters Uses Unsafe uint72/uint32 Casts Enabling Silent Parameter Truncation
threats.hypothesis_description: In changeTokenomicsParameters(), input parameters are cast to smaller types (uint72 for devsPerCapital/codePerDev, uint32 for epochLen) BEFORE being compared against bounds. For example, 'if (uint72(_devsPerCapital) > MIN_PARAM_VALUE)' truncates a uint256 to uint72 before comparison. If the owner (likely a DAO governance contract) passes a value like 2^72 + 1e15, the uint72 cast truncates to 1e15 which passes the MIN_PARAM_VALUE check, and the parameter is set to 1e15 instead of the intended large value. Similar truncation affects epochLen via uint32 cast.
threats.investigation_rationale: When parameters are set via DAO governance proposals, the proposal specifies uint256 values. If the proposal contains a value larger than the target type, it silently truncates without reverting. This could lead to unintended tokenomics parameter configurations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (uint72(_devsPerCapital) > MIN_PARAM_VALUE) { devsPerCapital = uint72(_devsPerCapital); } — cast before comparison allows values > 2^72 to truncate and pass the check

preconditions: Owner passes a uint256 value that exceeds the target type's range
The truncated value still passes the bounds check
Owner is a governance contract executing a proposal with the incorrect value

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unsafe downcasting of function parameters before validation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner-only function, but truncation is a code defect
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time changeTokenomicsParameters is called with oversized values

operational_questions: Has any governance proposal ever specified values larger than uint72/uint32 max?
What safeguards exist in the governance proposal creation process?
Should the function revert on overflow instead of silently truncating?
validation_requirements: Call changeTokenomicsParameters with _devsPerCapital = 2^72 + 1e15
Verify devsPerCapital is set to the truncated value without reverting
Confirm the truncated value passes all validation checks

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A - this is a code quality defect, not a direct attack vector
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: A governance misconfiguration could accidentally set parameters to truncated values
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Truncated parameters affect IDF calculation and epoch timing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Impact depends on which parameter is truncated and by how much
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires governance proposal with oversized parameter
