potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: IDF calculation and epoch timing could be affected
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires another governance proposal to correct

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Parameter values that differ significantly from governance proposal inputs
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected parameter values after governance updates

threats.title: Unrestricted nominee creation enables attacker-controlled staking target to siphon cross-chain staking emissions
threats.hypothesis_description: Because VoteWeighting allows anyone to add nominees, an attacker can list their own staking target (on some chainId) as a nominee and then use legitimate veOLAS voting (including their own veOLAS, or apathetic voter participation) to route a meaningful fraction of staking incentives toward a malicious staking contract that can immediately extract/redirect deposited OLAS.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is not a generic 'admin rug' scenario: the code explicitly makes listing permissionless, so operationally the key question is whether the downstream staking target verification (factory + verifier + dispenser/deposit processor config) is sufficient to prevent malicious staking contracts from being funded once they gain vote weight.
deployment_assumptions: VoteWeighting is deployed with permissionless addNominee* enabled (as in code)
Dispenser is set as VoteWeighting.dispenser and addNominee triggers Dispenser.addNominee to initialize claiming state
StakingFactory verifier policy (if any) allows attacker-created instances or is unset/weak
threats.config_evidence: No deployment scripts provided; on-chain configuration must be checked (VoteWeighting.dispenser, StakingFactory.verifier, StakingVerifier.implementationsCheck/whitelists).

proof_of_signal.pattern: addNomineeNonEVM is external with no OwnerOnly; voteForNomineeWeights updates gauge weights permissionlessly

preconditions: Attacker can create/point to a staking target address that can receive deposits and extract value (e.g., via permissive staking implementation behavior)
Attacker (or coalition) can obtain enough veOLAS voting power to assign non-trivial weight
Target passes (or bypasses) L2 verification path: stakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount(target) returns > 0 at time of L2 processing

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Nominee listing is permissionless; only later checks are offloaded to factory/verifier and operational configuration.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on verifier/whitelisting posture and whether Dispenser is configured to process newly added nominees automatically.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after nominee listing and once votes become effective for epochs being claimed.

operational_questions: Is StakingFactory.verifier set and enforcing implementation allowlists (implementationsCheck)?
Can an attacker deploy a staking proxy instance that passes verification but allows immediate withdrawal of deposits or mis-accounting?
Do deposit processors / L2 dispensers enforce additional allowlists beyond factory verification?
validation_requirements: Simulate creation of a staking instance via StakingFactory and see if it can pass verifier while being economically malicious
Trace end-to-end claimStakingIncentives -> bridge -> L2 dispenser -> staking.deposit to see who can ultimately withdraw OLAS

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy staking proxy instance via StakingFactory with a permissive implementation (or find an already enabled weak instance).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call VoteWeighting.addNomineeNonEVM/addNomineeEVM to list the target as a nominee.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Acquire veOLAS and voteForNomineeWeights to give the target measurable weight; wait for epoch(s).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call Dispenser.claimStakingIncentives for that nominee to trigger OLAS bridging and L2 deposit; extract OLAS via malicious staking target mechanics.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None required if verification is weak; emission routing can be captured economically.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Staking emission distribution diverted away from legitimate programs; reputational damage.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: requires DAO/owner action to remove nominee, tighten verifier/whitelists, and potentially recover funds from malicious staking target if possible.

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Sufficient veOLAS to earn non-trivial nomineeRelativeWeight (depends on participation).
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Epoch boundaries and vote delay constraints apply.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: New nominees being added frequently; nominees pointing to newly created staking proxy instances; abnormal concentration of votes.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rapid growth of staking incentives for a new nominee.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Votes concentrated from few veOLAS holders.

threats.title: Permissionless VoteWeighting checkpointing can be used to force-write gauge state and influence downstream incentive calculations
threats.hypothesis_description: Any account can call VoteWeighting.checkpointNominee(...) and nomineeRelativeWeightWrite(...), which advance stored weekly points (up to MAX_NUM_WEEKS). A motivated adversary can repeatedly force state transitions at chosen times to ensure particular time buckets are written, potentially affecting incentive computations that rely on these stored points (e.g., Dispenser using nomineeRelativeWeight at epoch endTime).
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A notes that downstream systems read weights at specific timestamps; permissionless writing means attackers can ensure state is filled (or partially filled) in ways that may change results for the same 'time' due to rounding / slope update ordering, especially near week boundaries.

proof_of_signal.pattern: checkpointNominee is external and calls internal _getWeight/_getSum which write pointsWeight/pointsSum forward week-over-week

preconditions: There exist nominees with non-zero weight/slope histories
Dispenser (or off-chain automation) relies on VoteWeighting stored points at specific times (e.g., epoch endTime) rather than recomputing from raw voteUserSlopes
Attacker can afford repeated calls (gas) to influence when state is materialized

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Gauge state is 'lazy-updated' by anyone; writes are time-dependent and capped by MAX_NUM_WEEKS.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Around week boundaries and before large claiming activity.

operational_questions: Do repeated checkpointNominee calls around boundary conditions change nomineeRelativeWeight outputs for the same time?
Can an attacker force a partial catch-up (MAX_NUM_WEEKS cap) that leaves stale points affecting relative weights?
validation_requirements: Property-based tests comparing relativeWeight results under different checkpointing schedules
Time-warp simulations across >53 weeks to observe drift/partial updates

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify high-emission nominees and their week buckets used for epoch endTime.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call checkpointNominee/nomineeRelativeWeightWrite strategically near week boundaries to force storage writes.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Trigger or wait for claimStakingIncentives calls that reference those timestamps; compare allocations vs baseline.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential skew in emission splits; higher variance and harder-to-audit distribution.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low/Medium: mostly accounting correctness; would require contract fixes if exploitable materially.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Sensitive to weekly rounding (WEEK) and epoch end timestamps.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High frequency of checkpointNominee/nomineeRelativeWeightWrite calls by unrelated accounts.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Relative weights changing unexpectedly without new votes.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated checkpoints concentrated on specific nominees.

threats.title: Asymmetric `effectiveBond` Adjustment Inflates Bond Capacity During Year Changes
threats.hypothesis_description: During year transitions where the annual inflation rate drops, `effectiveBond` is not reduced to account for the lower actual inflation, allowing the protocol to mint more OLAS for bonds than the inflation schedule permits.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `Tokenomics.checkpoint()`, `effectiveBond` is adjusted if the actual `maxBond` (`incentives[4]`) is greater than the estimated `curMaxBond`. However, there is no `else if (incentives[4] < curMaxBond)` to reduce `effectiveBond`. During year boundaries where inflation drops (e.g., Years 4 through 9), the actual inflation for the epoch will be less than the estimate. The missing downward adjustment leaves `effectiveBond` artificially inflated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Asymmetric state adjustment: `if (incentives[4] > curMaxBond)` lacks a corresponding downward adjustment branch.

preconditions: Epoch spans a year boundary where the new year's inflation rate is lower than the previous year's.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing downward adjustment for an estimated running total
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `effectiveBond` retains the overestimated `curMaxBond` value when actual inflation drops.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No `else if (incentives[4] < curMaxBond) { effectiveBond -= (curMaxBond - incentives[4]); }`.

analysis_questions: Does the inflation schedule strictly decrease between years 4 and 9?
Can `incentives[4]` be strictly less than `curMaxBond` during these transitions?
How much excess bond capacity is generated per year transition?
validation_requirements: Simulate an epoch transition across the Year 4 to Year 5 boundary.
Calculate the exact difference between `curMaxBond` and `incentives[4]`.
Verify `effectiveBond` is higher than the mathematically correct cumulative bond allowance.

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: As the protocol naturally crosses the boundary from Year 4 to Year 5 (where inflation drops from 80M to 72M), the `checkpoint` function calculates a lower actual `maxBond` but fails to reduce `effectiveBond`. The Depository can then issue bond programs using this phantom capacity, minting OLAS that exceeds the hardcoded inflation curve.
prerequisites: Protocol reaches a deflationary year boundary (Years 4-9)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Minting of unbacked OLAS tokens exceeding the intended inflation schedule.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All OLAS holders (dilution)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot be recovered once bonds are minted.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for epoch checkpoints spanning deflationary year boundaries.

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Specific block timestamps corresponding to year boundaries
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor `effectiveBond` vs theoretical max bond capacity.

threats.title: Native ETH Permanently Locked if `migrate` is Called Before `drain`
threats.hypothesis_description: The `migrate` function in L2 target dispensers transfers all OLAS tokens and permanently locks the contract by zeroing the owner. If the contract holds native ETH, it becomes permanently trapped because `drain` can no longer be called.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `DefaultTargetDispenserL2`, `migrate` sets `owner = address(0)` and `_locked = 2`. The contract can receive native ETH via `receive()` (used for cross-chain refunds or accidental transfers). The only way to recover this ETH is via `drain()`, which requires `msg.sender == owner`. If an admin executes `migrate` without first calling `drain`, the native ETH is permanently locked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Irreversible privilege revocation without sweeping all asset types: `owner = address(0)` leaves `address(this).balance` stranded.

preconditions: L2 Dispenser contract holds native ETH (e.g., from bridge refunds).
Admin calls `migrate` before calling `drain`.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incomplete asset migration
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Native ETH is ignored during migration and locked due to owner revocation.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No automatic transfer of `address(this).balance` inside `migrate`.

analysis_questions: How often do L2 dispensers accumulate native ETH?
Is there any fallback mechanism to recover funds from an ownerless contract?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to the dispenser, call `migrate`, and verify the ETH cannot be recovered.

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During a protocol upgrade, the DAO votes to migrate to a new L2 dispenser. The execution payload calls `migrate(newAddress)`. The contract successfully moves all OLAS and locks itself. However, accumulated bridge refunds in native ETH are left behind and permanently lost.
prerequisites: Contract migration event
Native ETH balance > 0

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of native ETH held by the contract.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol treasury
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Impossible.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying complete asset recovery during migration.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Check native balances of migrated dispensers.

threats.title: `VoteWeighting` Checkpoint Stagnation if Missed for Over 53 Weeks
threats.hypothesis_description: If a nominee or the global sum is not checkpointed for more than 53 weeks, the state advancement loop completes without updating the timestamp, causing an infinite loop of stagnation where the state can never catch up to the present.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `VoteWeighting._getSum()` and `_getWeight()`, a loop advances the state week-by-week up to `MAX_NUM_WEEKS` (53). The timestamp `timeSum` is only updated if `t > block.timestamp`. If the time elapsed since the last checkpoint is > 53 weeks, the loop finishes 53 iterations, `t` remains `<= block.timestamp`, and `timeSum` is never updated. Subsequent calls will start from the exact same old `timeSum`, recalculate the same 53 weeks, and again fail to advance, permanently bricking the state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop bound prevents state progression: `if (t > block.timestamp) { timeSum = t; }` is unreachable if elapsed time > `MAX_NUM_WEEKS`.

preconditions: No checkpoint is called for a specific nominee or the global sum for > 53 weeks.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Flawed pagination/catch-up logic
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `timeSum` and `timeWeight` are not updated if the loop hits its maximum iterations before reaching the current time.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No unconditional update of `timeSum = t` at the end of the loop.

analysis_questions: Can an attacker intentionally brick a specific nominee by creating it and ignoring it for 54 weeks?
Does bricking a single nominee affect the global `timeSum`?
validation_requirements: Deploy contract, wait 54 weeks, call `checkpoint()`, and observe that `timeSum` does not advance.

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker adds a dummy nominee and does not vote for it. After 54 weeks, the attacker calls `checkpointNominee`. The nominee's `timeWeight` fails to advance. While this only bricks the dummy nominee, if the entire protocol is paused or inactive for 54 weeks, the global `timeSum` becomes permanently bricked, halting all voting and reward distribution.
prerequisites: 54 weeks of inactivity for a specific state variable

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS of gauge weight calculations.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for extreme time advancement (> 1 year) between checkpoints.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Check for `timeSum` values significantly lagging behind `block.timestamp`.

threats.title: DefaultTargetDispenserL2.syncWithheldTokens() can be griefed via bridge-level revert after zeroing withheldAmount (state-loss if _sendMessage does not revert)
threats.hypothesis_description: syncWithheldTokens() sets withheldAmount = 0 before calling the abstract _sendMessage(). If a concrete _sendMessage implementation can fail without reverting (e.g., returns false-like behavior, swallows errors, or calls a bridge that does not revert but drops the message), the contract may lose accounting for withheld tokens, breaking withheld_amount_conservation across L1/L2.
threats.investigation_rationale: In the provided implementations, several _sendMessage paths rely on external bridge calls and cost quoting; most revert on insufficient msg.value. However, correctness depends on bridge behavior and SDK functions; if any bridge call can fail silently, the ordering (effects before interactions) becomes dangerous for accounting integrity.
deployment_assumptions: At least one deployed L2 TargetDispenser implementation uses an _sendMessage that can fail without reverting (or can be forced into a non-reverting failure mode)
Operators/users call syncWithheldTokens() with parameters that trigger this failure mode
threats.config_evidence: DefaultTargetDispenserL2.syncWithheldTokens() zeros withheldAmount prior to _sendMessage(amount, bridgePayload). Concrete implementations are external-call heavy (e.g., WormholeTargetDispenserL2, OptimismTargetDispenserL2, GnosisTargetDispenserL2, ArbitrumTargetDispenserL2, PolygonTargetDispenserL2).

proof_of_signal.pattern: state update (zeroing) before external bridge call; assumes external call reverts on failure

preconditions: withheldAmount > 0
_sendMessage does not revert despite failing to deliver/record the message on L1
No alternative mechanism restores withheldAmount on failure

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: effects-before-interactions with an external system that may have non-reverting failures
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: loss of withheldAmount accounting causes L1 to never credit withheld amounts; future transfers not offset; protocol accounting drift
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no try/catch with restoration, no confirmation callback, no sequence persistence in state

analysis_questions: Do any concrete _sendMessage implementations use bridge functions that can fail silently (return value ignored) or be non-reverting on non-delivery?
Is there any on-chain confirmation that the message was actually enqueued (sequence stored)?
Are there compensating admin/maintenance functions to restore withheldAmount on failure?
validation_requirements: Review each _sendMessage implementation for return values and revert guarantees
Simulate failure modes with mocked bridge contracts that return without reverting
Check monitoring/ops procedures: do they assume revert on failure?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker (or simply a faulty bridge) causes _sendMessage to not deliver while not reverting; withheldAmount is cleared; protocol loses the credit that should offset future L1 emissions, effectively burning that portion of emissions or breaking L1/L2 reconciliation.
prerequisites: Bridge/API that can fail non-reverting
Ability to induce that failure mode (e.g., wrong payload formatting accepted but dropped)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Material emissions/accounting loss; could cause systematic underfunding or mis-reporting of staking incentives
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All stakers on that L2 and L1 emission reconciliation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (DAO maintenance can potentially re-sync amounts if off-chain tracked)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for non-reverting bridge failure modes and withheldAmount restoration

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: compare WithheldAmountSynced events vs L1 sync receipts; look for missing L1 updates

threats.title: Tokenomics.trackServiceDonations() does not validate `amounts.length == serviceIds.length`, enabling treasury-originated misaccounting or unexpected reverts inside _trackServiceDonations
threats.hypothesis_description: trackServiceDonations() iterates over serviceIds to validate existence, but never checks amounts array length matches serviceIds length before passing both into _trackServiceDonations(). Inside _trackServiceDonations(), it indexes amounts[i]. If amounts is shorter, the call reverts; if longer, extra amounts are ignored. While only treasury can call trackServiceDonations(), a bug/misconfiguration or compromised treasury could cause donation accounting DoS or partial incentive allocation inconsistent with donationETH recorded.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete input validation omission in an externally callable state-changing function. Even if the caller is privileged (treasury), it is an integration point; a single malformed call can revert and block donation tracking for a block/epoch, or create inconsistencies between totalDonationsETH and per-unit pending rewards.
deployment_assumptions: Treasury integration might pass mismatched arrays due to bug, upgrade, or malicious behavior
Protocol relies on successful donation tracking for correct incentive accrual
threats.config_evidence: Tokenomics.trackServiceDonations() comment claims treasury validates arrays; code does not enforce it.

proof_of_signal.lines: trackServiceDonations(): loops `for i < serviceIds.length` then calls _trackServiceDonations(donator, serviceIds, amounts, curEpoch) without checking lengths; _trackServiceDonations uses `amounts[i]`
proof_of_signal.pattern: missing array length equality check across correlated arrays

preconditions: Caller is treasury (required)
serviceIds.length != amounts.length

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: no WrongArrayLength check despite parallel arrays
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: revert/partial accounting leads to donation DoS or mismatch between donationETH and per-unit allocations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no `if (serviceIds.length != amounts.length) revert WrongArrayLength(...)`

analysis_questions: Does Treasury always guarantee correct lengths on-chain, or is it off-chain assumed only?
Can mismatched arrays lead to state partially updated before revert (e.g., totalDonationsETH already increased)?
Is there any other guard that makes this impossible in practice?
validation_requirements: Unit test: call trackServiceDonations() as treasury with mismatched arrays; observe whether totalDonationsETH write happens before revert (it does) and confirm revert undoes it
Review Treasury implementation for length checks and atomicity

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised treasury submits mismatched arrays repeatedly, causing consistent reverts and preventing donation tracking; if combined with other operations, may stall developer incentive updates tied to donations.
prerequisites: Treasury privilege or treasury bug

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate disruption of donation-based incentive accrual; no direct theft
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Service/component/agent owners expecting donation-funded rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High (fix in treasury or tokenomics; governance action)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No negative tests for mismatched donation arrays

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor reverts of trackServiceDonations and check for off-chain treasury payload formation bugs

threats.title: Tokenomics incentive finalization can revert due to division by zero when sumUnitTopUpsOLAS == 0 but pendingRelativeTopUp > 0 (state-corruption edge between epochs)
threats.hypothesis_description: In _finalizeIncentivesForUnitId(), topUp calculation divides by `sumUnitIncentives = sumUnitTopUpsOLAS * 100`. If sumUnitTopUpsOLAS for the epoch/unitType is zero while a unit has nonzero pendingRelativeTopUp, finalization will revert (division by zero). Although the code attempts to only add pendingRelativeTopUp when also incrementing sumUnitTopUpsOLAS, cross-epoch state updates and partial finalization paths may leave inconsistent state (e.g., storage corruption, manual maintenance calls, or future code changes). If reachable, it can block accountOwnerIncentives() and/or future donation tracking that triggers finalization.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a high-impact DoS if reachable because accountOwnerIncentives() is called by Dispenser for user claims and finalization can also occur during donation tracking. Phase A flagged finalization as an invariant-sensitive area with uint96 casts; division-by-zero here would brick claims for affected unitIds.
deployment_assumptions: There exists a reachable state where mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].pendingRelativeTopUp > 0 but mapEpochTokenomics[epochNum].unitPoints[unitType].sumUnitTopUpsOLAS == 0
Such state can be created via integration bugs, storage migration, or an edge-case in donation tracking / epoch copying
threats.config_evidence: No explicit guard `require(sumUnitIncentives > 0)` before division; relies on implicit coupling between pendingRelativeTopUp and sumUnitTopUpsOLAS increments in _trackServiceDonations().

proof_of_signal.lines: _finalizeIncentivesForUnitId(): `sumUnitIncentives = uint256(sumUnitTopUpsOLAS) * 100; totalIncentives / sumUnitIncentives` with no zero check
proof_of_signal.pattern: division using epoch-wide accumulator without explicit nonzero guard

preconditions: A unit has pendingRelativeTopUp > 0 for some lastEpoch
For that lastEpoch and unitType, sumUnitTopUpsOLAS == 0
Finalization is triggered via accountOwnerIncentives() or _trackServiceDonations() calling _finalizeIncentivesForUnitId()

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: division by `sumUnitTopUpsOLAS * 100` without requiring it > 0
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: if accumulator is zero, owner claiming incentives reverts and cannot clear balances
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no conditional branch when sumUnitTopUpsOLAS == 0 (e.g., treat as zero top-up)

analysis_questions: Can pendingRelativeTopUp ever be nonzero without incrementing sumUnitTopUpsOLAS in the same epoch (including via partial loops, reverts, or future upgrades)?
Does epoch parameter copying ever reset sumUnitTopUpsOLAS while keeping unit pendingRelativeTopUp?
Are there any maintenance/admin functions (not shown) that can mutate these values independently?
validation_requirements: Property-based fuzzing of donation tracking and claiming across multiple epochs to search for states where sumUnitTopUpsOLAS==0 but pendingRelativeTopUp>0
Review full codebase for any direct writes to mapUnitIncentives.pendingRelativeTopUp or mapEpochTokenomics.sumUnitTopUpsOLAS besides _trackServiceDonations()

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a malformed maintenance call or a buggy treasury donation submission creates inconsistent top-up state, an attacker holding affected unitId can force reverts in incentive-claim paths (or permanently prevent claim) until governance repairs state.
prerequisites: Inconsistent state creation (bug/migration/admin mistake) or unforeseen path

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High operational impact: blocked claims and stalled incentive distribution for affected units
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Owners of affected components/agents; potentially all claimers if common accumulator is corrupted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (requires governance/state repair if possible)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No invariant/fuzz tests linking pendingRelativeTopUp and sumUnitTopUpsOLAS across epochs

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor claim reverts and inspect epoch accumulators for zero denominators

threats.title: StakingToken.deposit() updates accounting before transferFrom, enabling balance/availableRewards inflation if stakingToken is non-standard (reentrant or returns true but transfers less)
threats.hypothesis_description: StakingToken.deposit() increments `balance` and `availableRewards` before calling SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(). If the stakingToken is a non-standard ERC20 that can re-enter (e.g., ERC777 hooks or malicious token) or otherwise cause the transfer to behave unexpectedly (e.g., transfer less than amount but still returns success through crafted return data), internal accounting may temporarily or permanently diverge from actual token balances, potentially violating staking solvency invariants and allowing over-allocation of rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: Project docs explicitly state the system is expected to interact with 'weird' ERC20s. While SafeTransferLib checks return values, it cannot guarantee the token transferred the full amount (it only checks call success/return data). Updating accounting before the transfer magnifies risk if the token is adversarial or has callbacks.
deployment_assumptions: A stakingToken instance is configured to use an ERC20 with non-standard semantics (rebasing, fee-on-transfer, callback hooks, or malicious behavior)
Attackers can call deposit() with such a token approved
threats.config_evidence: StakingToken.stakingToken is set during initialize() without on-chain enforcement of standard ERC20 behavior; docs mention weird tokens. deposit() performs effects before interaction.

proof_of_signal.lines: deposit(): `balance = balance + amount; availableRewards = availableRewards + amount;` then `SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(...)`
proof_of_signal.pattern: effects-before-interactions; ERC20 transferFrom cannot be assumed to transfer exact amount

preconditions: stakingToken behaves non-standardly such that actual received amount < `amount` or triggers re-entrancy/callback side effects
Protocol uses internal `balance/availableRewards` for reward allocation and withdrawals (it does in StakingBase)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: accounting updated before verifying actual token receipt
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: insolvency: contract believes it has more rewards than real token balance, leading to later withdrawal failures
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no pre/post balance delta check (balanceOf) to assert actual receipt; no nonReentrant on deposit

analysis_questions: Are stakingToken addresses constrained to known-good ERC20s in production (e.g., via StakingVerifier token check)?
Can deposit() be called with fee-on-transfer tokens that transfer less but still return success?
Can ERC777-style callbacks re-enter checkpoint/unstake/claim flows to exploit temporary inflated availableRewards?
validation_requirements: Test with fee-on-transfer token where transferFrom moves (amount - fee) and returns true; observe accounting divergence
Test with malicious token that re-enters during transferFrom (if possible) and calls checkpoint/claim/unstake
Add invariant tests for balanceOf(stakingToken) >= balance and for INV-ECON-001

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits using a fee-on-transfer token: internal availableRewards increases by `amount`, but contract receives less. Later, during checkpoint reward allocation, rewards are computed based on inflated availableRewards, and withdrawals may fail or allocate unbacked rewards, causing DoS or economic inconsistencies.
prerequisites: Non-standard staking token configured
Attacker can deposit

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High: staking contract insolvency or inability to pay promised rewards; possible permanent reward loss/DoS
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All stakers in that staking instance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (requires token replacement/migration or top-up)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for fee-on-transfer/rebasing/erc777-like tokens against deposit accounting

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: low (small deposits can cause accounting drift)
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: single transaction for accounting drift; multi-epoch to realize failures
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: compare internal `balance` vs ERC20.balanceOf(this) over time

threats.title: StakingNativeToken._withdraw() can be weaponized for DoS by multisig recipients that intentionally revert on receiving ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: StakingNativeToken._withdraw() uses `to.call{value: amount}("")` and reverts if the call fails. If the service multisig address is a contract that rejects ETH (reverts/fallback consumes too much gas), then any claim/unstake that tries to pay rewards will revert, potentially preventing service owners from unstaking (if reward > 0) and blocking reward claims indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: Service multisig is sourced from ServiceRegistry and validated by proxyHash, but that does not guarantee it can receive ETH. Since the protocol uses native token rewards for this staking variant, a misconfigured or malicious multisig implementation can brick exits/claims for that service.
deployment_assumptions: A staking instance uses StakingNativeToken and pays native rewards
A service is registered with a multisig contract that cannot receive ETH (or later upgraded to reject ETH) but still matches proxyHash checks at stake time
threats.config_evidence: StakingBase.stake() validates multisig code hash equals proxyHash, but does not validate payable receive/fallback behavior. StakingNativeToken._withdraw() reverts on failed call.

proof_of_signal.lines: _withdraw(): `(bool success,) = to.call{value: amount}(""); if (!success) revert TransferFailed(...)`
proof_of_signal.pattern: hard-revert on native transfer failure to recipient contract

preconditions: Service multisig address is a contract with non-payable receive/fallback or intentionally reverting logic
Service has nonzero reward when trying to claim/unstake

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: recipient-controlled revert blocks protocol flow
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: service owner may be unable to claim rewards or unstake if reward payout always reverts
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no alternative withdrawal mechanism (pull payments) and no ability to redirect payout address

analysis_questions: Are multisig implementations guaranteed payable across all supported deployments?
Can multisig be upgraded after staking such that it begins reverting on ETH receipt while code hash remains constant (proxy)?
Does unstake() revert only when reward>0; can owners zero reward first?
validation_requirements: Test: stake with a multisig proxy that delegates to implementation that reverts on receive; attempt claim/unstake
Review service registry multisig upgradeability model and whether proxyHash check can be bypassed by proxy design

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A service operator (or attacker who convinces/controls multisig) uses a multisig that cannot receive ETH; service accrues rewards; later any claim/unstake reverts, locking the service NFT in the staking contract and making rewards unclaimable.
prerequisites: Misconfigured or malicious multisig contract
Native-token staking instance in use

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium: locks service NFT and/or blocks reward payout for affected services
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Service owners using native-token staking variant
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (may require service registry migration or manual intervention depending on NFT custody rules)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for non-payable multisig recipients in native reward withdrawals

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: recipient call gas behavior and revert conditions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor TransferFailed reverts during claims/unstakes

threats.title: Immediate State Update of Tokenomics Parameters Breaks Epoch Isolation
threats.hypothesis_description: Changes to `devsPerCapital`, `codePerDev`, and `epsilonRate` update state variables immediately, rather than being deferred to the next epoch like other parameters (`nextEpochLen`, `nextVeOLASThreshold`). This allows an admin to manipulate the current epoch's Inverse Discount Factor (IDF) and reward distributions retroactively before `checkpoint` is called.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol's design intent (stated in comments) is that parameter changes take effect in the next epoch to prevent mid-epoch manipulation. Immediate updates break this isolation, allowing retroactive changes to already-accumulated donations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State variables updated immediately instead of using next* variables

preconditions: Admin calls changeTokenomicsParameters mid-epoch

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin observes donations, changes parameters to maximize/minimize IDF for the current epoch
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Admin can retroactively alter the split between treasury and component/agent rewards for already-received donations

economic_questions: How much can the IDF be manipulated mid-epoch?
What is the maximum financial impact on component/agent rewards?
validation_requirements: Simulate mid-epoch parameter changes and calculate the difference in finalized rewards

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Variable depending on total epoch donations

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Shifts value between Treasury and unit owners

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: IDF calculation sensitivity to devsPerCapital and codePerDev
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare intended deferred update vs actual immediate update

threats.title: Wormhole Bridge Decimal Normalization Mismatch Causes Permanent Dust Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: Withheld amounts synced from L2 to L1 are not normalized on L1. When deducted from a normalized `transferAmount` during `claimStakingIncentives`, the resulting amount becomes un-normalized. When sent via the Wormhole bridge (which truncates to 8 decimals), the dust is permanently stuck in the `WormholeDepositProcessorL1` contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a systematic accounting leak. Because the Wormhole bridge silently truncates tokens to 8 decimals, any un-normalized dust resulting from the deduction of L2 withheld amounts will be permanently locked in the L1 deposit processor.
deployment_assumptions: Wormhole bridge is used for a target chain
threats.config_evidence: WormholeDepositProcessorL1.sol uses sendTokenWithPayloadToEvm which truncates to 8 decimals

proof_of_signal.pattern: transferAmount -= withheldAmount where withheldAmount is not normalized

preconditions: Target chain uses Wormhole (bridgingDecimals = 8)
A staking target on L2 has a limitAmount with dust (non-zero in last 10 decimals)
The withheld amount is synced to L1 and deducted from a future transfer

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploying a staking contract with a dusty rewardsPerSecond to force dusty withheld amounts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Systematic permanent locking of OLAS dust in the L1 deposit processor

economic_questions: How much dust can be locked per transaction?
Does the locked dust eventually cause insolvency in the Dispenser?
validation_requirements: Simulate a dusty withheld amount sync and subsequent claim via Wormhole
Check the balance of WormholeDepositProcessorL1 after the transfer

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 10^10 wei per transaction locked

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Slow deflation of circulating OLAS due to permanent lock

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative dust over thousands of transactions

threats.title: VoteWeighting changesSum Underflow DoS After Nominee Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: After removeNominee zeroes a nominee's bias but does NOT clean up future scheduled slope changes in changesSum/changesWeight, the _getSum() internal function can underflow when processing those residual slope decrements. Since _getSum() is called by voteForNomineeWeights, checkpointNominee, and nomineeRelativeWeightWrite, the entire VoteWeighting system (and thus staking incentive distribution) can be permanently DoS'd.
threats.investigation_rationale: In removeNominee, pointsSum[nextTime].bias is set to newSum but pointsSum[nextTime].slope is NOT adjusted. Users who voted for the removed nominee have future changesSum entries (at their lock expiration). When _getSum() processes weeks where bias reaches 0 (resetting slope to 0 via the if/else guard), subsequent changesSum decrements try to subtract from zero slope, causing Solidity 0.8+ underflow revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getSum() performs pt.slope -= dSlope without underflow protection; removeNominee zeros bias but not slope, leaving residual changesSum entries from unrevoked votes

preconditions: A nominee with active votes is removed via removeNominee
Voters do NOT call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower
Enough time passes for bias decay to zero slope via the if/else guard in _getSum
A changesSum entry from an unrevoked vote tries to subtract from now-zero slope

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _getSum: pt.slope -= dSlope where dSlope includes removed nominee voter slopes, but pt.slope was zeroed when bias hit 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner removes nominee (normal admin action); users naturally fail to revoke votes; system DoSes itself over time
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit - this is a protocol-breaking DoS that halts all staking incentive distribution and voting

economic_questions: How quickly does bias decay reach zero after nominee removal?
What percentage of users typically revoke votes from removed nominees?
Can the DoS be triggered intentionally by an attacker who votes then doesn't revoke?
validation_requirements: Simulate: add nominee, vote, remove nominee, advance time past lock expiration, call _getSum
Verify that pt.slope -= dSlope reverts when slope was zeroed by bias decay
Test if revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower by ALL voters prevents the issue

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal veOLAS to cast a vote (~100 OLAS locked)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for voting + waiting for admin to remove nominee
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; protocol-wide disruption of all staking incentive distribution

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After any nominee removal where voters don't revoke
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete halt of staking incentive claims across all chains

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All veOLAS voters and staking incentive claimants
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete inability to vote or claim staking incentives
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire staking incentive allocation per epoch

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace _getSum through weeks after nominee removal with unrevoked votes

threats.title: Dispenser retain() Uses Stale Retainer Weight Due to Missing Checkpoint
threats.hypothesis_description: The retain() function in Dispenser queries nomineeRelativeWeight for the retainer without first calling checkpointNominee. Unlike calculateStakingIncentives which explicitly checkpoints, retain() reads potentially stale weight data. This causes incorrect return amounts, leaving OLAS in the staking inflation pool instead of being properly returned.
threats.investigation_rationale: In calculateStakingIncentives: IVoteWeighting(voteWeighting).checkpointNominee(stakingTarget, chainId) is called before querying weights. In retain(): only nomineeRelativeWeight(retainer, block.chainid, endTime) is called - a view function that reads stored points without updating them. If the retainer's weight hasn't been checkpointed recently, the points data is stale.

proof_of_signal.pattern: retain() calls nomineeRelativeWeight (view) without prior checkpointNominee; calculateStakingIncentives at line 584 calls checkpointNominee before weight queries

preconditions: Retainer has votes allocated to it in VoteWeighting
No one has called checkpointNominee for the retainer recently
retain() is called, reading stale weight data

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalReturnAmount += stakingPoint.stakingIncentive * stakingWeight where stakingWeight is stale
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Stale weight = lower/zero weight → less OLAS returned to inflation → staking incentives under-refunded
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: OLAS that should be returned to inflation is lost, reducing future epoch staking allocations

economic_questions: How stale can the retainer's weight become between checkpoints?

threats.title: Unprivileged caller can add arbitrary nominees to VoteWeighting, forcing Dispenser.addNominee() and potentially freezing staking incentive claiming via mass nominee spam
threats.hypothesis_description: VoteWeighting allows permissionless nominee creation (addNomineeEVM/addNomineeNonEVM). When a Dispenser is configured, VoteWeighting auto-calls Dispenser.addNominee(nomineeHash), which writes mapLastClaimedStakingEpochs[nomineeHash] = epochCounter. A spammer can create a very large nominee set, increasing operational overhead and potentially making routine checkpointing / claiming workflows impractical (off-chain processes that enumerate nominees, governance dashboards, or operational scripts). This is particularly impactful because claiming requires the nominee to have been added (mapLastClaimedStakingEpochs != 0), so the system’s nominee lifecycle is tightly coupled.

threats.title: Tokenomics.checkpoint() Lacks Reentrancy Guard Despite External Call to Treasury Before Epoch Increment
threats.hypothesis_description: The checkpoint() function makes an external call to ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) BEFORE incrementing epochCounter. threats.title: L2 target dispenser redeem path can bypass updated staking target validity because it does not re-verify against factory limits
threats.hypothesis_description: Queued deposits on L2 (created when paused or insufficient OLAS balance) can later be redeemed via redeem(target, amount, batchNonce). This redeem does not re-check verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount(target). If a target was valid at queue time but later disabled or limited, redeem can still deposit the queued amount to it, bypassing the intended ongoing validity policy.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A already highlights this as an invariant risk; operationally, it matters if targets can become invalid over time (deployer disables instance; verifier policy tightened; emissions limit lowered), yet queued items still execute.
deployment_assumptions: Targets can change validity between queue and redeem (e.g., StakingFactory instance disabled by deployer or verifier policy changed)
The L2 dispenser holds enough OLAS later to redeem
threats.config_evidence: DefaultTargetDispenserL2.redeem checks only stakingQueueingNonces[queueHash] and balance; no call to stakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount. Validity checks occur only in _processData.

proof_of_signal.pattern: redeem() performs approve+deposit after only queueHash check; no verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount call

preconditions: A queued entry exists for (target, amount, batchNonce) with stakingQueueingNonces[queueHash] == true
Target becomes invalid or should be capped differently after queue time
Contract later becomes unpaused and has sufficient OLAS balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Two-step execution: verification at queue time only, execution at redeem time without re-verification.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Partially depends on whether target validity can change (deployer-only disable, verifier updates).

operational_questions: Can a target be disabled by deployer after queueing and still be economically harmful if it receives queued deposits?
Are queued entries expected to persist for long durations in production (paused states, balance deficits)?
validation_requirements: Create queued item; then disable instance via StakingFactory.setInstanceStatus; then call redeem and confirm deposit still happens
Check if any L2 staking implementations allow immediate withdrawal/redirect of deposited OLAS

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Ensure deposits are queued (pause dispenser or induce insufficient OLAS balance).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Change target validity (disable instance or change verifier limits).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call redeem to force deposit to now-invalid target.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Potential emission leakage to targets that should have been defunded.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Undermines trust in ability to deactivate targets; funds can still flow via queue backlog.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: requires manual queue management or migration.

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None if attacker can benefit as the target operator; otherwise requires target operator collusion.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large stakingQueueingNonces backlog; redeems occurring long after original MessageReceived; deposits to targets recently disabled.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Deposits executed despite governance action to disable targets.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Targets disabled shortly before redeem spikes.

threats.title: Any address can call StakingToken.initialize / StakingNativeToken.initialize first on a freshly deployed implementation, permanently capturing configuration
threats.hypothesis_description: StakingToken.initialize and StakingNativeToken.initialize are external and have no access control; they rely only on single-init guard inside StakingBase._initialize (serviceRegistry != address(0)). If an implementation contract is deployed standalone (not behind a proxy) or a proxy is deployed but not initialized atomically, an attacker can initialize it first, setting critical staking parameters (serviceRegistry, activityChecker, proxyHash, rewardsPerSecond, etc.) and effectively hijacking the staking contract configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: While standard deployments should initialize immediately, this remains an operational risk for factories, migrations, or manual deployments. The impact includes bogus staking pools that appear legitimate but route rewards or stake NFTs under attacker-controlled conditions.
deployment_assumptions: A staking proxy instance is deployed and left uninitialized for at least one transaction, OR an implementation is used directly
Attackers can discover the address (on-chain) and call initialize before operators
threats.config_evidence: StakingToken.initialize / StakingNativeToken.initialize are external and unrestricted; only guard is in _initialize: if (serviceRegistry != address(0)) revert AlreadyInitialized().

proof_of_signal.pattern: function initialize(...) external { _initialize(_stakingParams); ... } (no OwnerOnly)

preconditions: Uninitialized staking instance exists at a known address
Operator did not initialize atomically within the deployment transaction bundle
Downstream systems (VoteWeighting nominees / L2 dispenser) will fund the instance once it gains weight

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Initializer is permissionless; safe only if deployment/initialization is atomic and consistent.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None for attacker once an uninitialized instance exists.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediately after deployment and before initialization.

operational_questions: Does StakingFactory.createStakingInstance always initialize in the same transaction (it does via instance.call(initPayload)) and is it the only deployment path?
Are there any scripts/processes that deploy staking proxies without calling initialize in the same transaction?
validation_requirements: Review deployment tooling; ensure no standalone staking instances are deployed uninitialized
Scan chain for staking proxy instances with serviceRegistry == address(0)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for newly created staking proxy instances (InstanceCreated events) and check if initialized.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If uninitialized, call initialize with attacker-chosen parameters.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Optionally list as nominee and vote to direct emissions.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Capture of staking pool configuration; can lead to misdirected incentives or malicious activity checking.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users staking services could be trapped in attacker-configured pool parameters.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: instance may need to be disabled (but only deployer can disable) or abandoned.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Service staking programs deployed via factory or manual ops

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires uninitialized window (should be rare if using factory correctly).

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Staking proxy instances with default/zeroed parameters; initialize calls not from expected deployer.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected activityChecker/serviceRegistry addresses in new staking pools.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid initialize tx following proxy deployment.

threats.title: ActivityChecker staticcall failure defaults to 'inactive', enabling griefing/DoS on reward distribution and evictions if checker is fragile
threats.hypothesis_description: StakingBase._checkRatioPass uses low-level staticcall to activityChecker.getMultisigNonces and isRatioPass, and if calls fail or return malformed data, ratioPass remains false. If ActivityChecker implementation is fragile (reverts for certain multisig types, returns dynamic data unexpectedly) or if multisig contracts behave unexpectedly, services can be treated as inactive, leading to loss of rewards and possible eviction after maxInactivityDuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is operationally practical: integrations with different multisig implementations or upgrades can break liveness checks and cascade into mass evictions and halted rewards for honest stakers.
deployment_assumptions: activityChecker address can be set to an implementation that is not robust across all service multisig contracts
Service multisig contracts may be non-standard or upgraded
threats.config_evidence: StakingBase._initialize requires activityChecker.code.length > 0 but does not enforce interface correctness; _checkRatioPass treats failure as ratioPass=false.

proof_of_signal.pattern: activityChecker.staticcall(...); if not success or wrong returndata => ratioPass stays false

preconditions: At least one staked service uses a multisig whose nonce() call or ratio check causes revert/malformed return
Checkpointing continues over multiple periods so inactivity accumulates past threshold

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Failure mode is 'silent inactive' rather than reverting or isolating a faulty service.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on activityChecker selection and compatibility with multisig implementations.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after services are staked; especially after upgrades to multisig/activityChecker.

operational_questions: Which multisig implementations are used by services and are they all compatible with the activity checker?
Is there an emergency mechanism to prevent eviction during checker outages?
validation_requirements: Fuzz activityChecker returnData shapes; verify behavior for reverts/short returns
Test with representative multisig implementations to ensure nonce() is stable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Introduce or encounter a multisig type that causes activity checker calls to fail.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Services get evicted and stop receiving rewards despite being active off-chain.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Mass reward denial and evictions; undermines staking reliability.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: may require migrating staking program or updating checker via new deployments.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Service staking rewards and eviction logic

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low/Medium (if attacker can choose multisig type; otherwise accidental integration failure).

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Spike in ServiceInactivityWarning / ServicesEvicted events after checker or multisig upgrades.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: ratioPass false for all services; nonces arrays empty/unexpected sizes.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Concentration of failures for certain multisig codehashes.

threats.title: Potential Reentrancy in `checkpoint` via Treasury Rebalance
threats.hypothesis_description: The `checkpoint` function makes an external call to the Treasury contract before incrementing the `epochCounter`. If the Treasury is compromised, it could re-enter `checkpoint` to duplicate epoch settlement.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `Tokenomics.checkpoint()`, `ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1])` is called. The `epochCounter` is only incremented if this call succeeds. There is no `_locked` reentrancy guard on `checkpoint`. If the Treasury contract re-enters `checkpoint`, the `epochCounter` is still at `eCounter`, and the same epoch data will be processed again, potentially duplicating inflation minting or corrupting state.
deployment_assumptions: Treasury contract is malicious, compromised, or contains a fallback that allows reentrancy.
threats.config_evidence: Treasury is an external contract set by the owner.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External call before state update: `rebalanceTreasury` called before `epochCounter = uint32(eCounter + 1);`.

preconditions: Treasury contract must be capable of re-entering the Tokenomics contract.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: CEI violation without reentrancy guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `epochCounter` is updated after the external call, allowing the same epoch to be processed multiple times.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No `_locked` check in `checkpoint()`.

analysis_questions: Does the `SameBlockNumberViolation` check prevent reentrancy in the same block?
Wait, `lastDonationBlockNumber` is only updated in `trackServiceDonations`, not `checkpoint`. So it does not prevent reentrancy.
validation_requirements: Create a mock malicious Treasury, trigger `checkpoint`, and verify if `epochCounter` increments twice or state is corrupted.

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised Treasury contract re-enters `checkpoint()`. Since `epochCounter` hasn't incremented, the contract recalculates `inflationPerEpoch` and credits `effectiveBond` again, double-counting the inflation for the epoch and allowing excess bonds to be minted.
prerequisites: Compromised or malicious Treasury contract

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Duplication of epoch inflation and bond capacity.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol tokenomics
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires fixing the Treasury and upgrading Tokenomics.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing reentrancy tests for `checkpoint`.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor for multiple `EpochSettled` events in the same transaction.

threats.title: Tokenomics changeIncentiveFractions Allows Zero Staking Parameters Propagation
threats.hypothesis_description: When changeIncentiveFractions is called, it sets bit 0x02 of tokenomicsParametersUpdated. During checkpoint, if bit 0x02 is set but bit 0x08 is NOT, the staking parameters (maxStakingIncentive, minStakingWeight) for the next epoch are copied from the current epoch. However, if changeStakingParams was NEVER called for the current epoch, these values might be zero (default), resulting in zero maxStakingIncentive which caps all staking distributions to zero.
threats.investigation_rationale: mapEpochStakingPoints[eCounter + 1].maxStakingIncentive defaults to 0 in storage. If it was never explicitly set by changeStakingParams, and only changeIncentiveFractions was called, the copy in checkpoint propagates from current epoch where it might also be unset. This could silently disable all staking incentive distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: When bit 0x02 set but 0x08 not set, maxStakingIncentive/minStakingWeight copied from current epoch; if current epoch values are zero (never initialized), next epoch inherits zeros

preconditions: Owner calls changeIncentiveFractions with nonzero stakingFraction
Owner does NOT call changeStakingParams to set maxStakingIncentive/minStakingWeight
The current epoch has zero maxStakingIncentive (not yet initialized)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin misconfiguration: setting staking fraction without corresponding staking params
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All staking incentives capped at 0 despite nonzero stakingFraction allocation

economic_questions: What are the initial values of maxStakingIncentive after deployment?
Is there a scenario where owner updates fractions but forgets staking params?
What is the recovery path once this configuration error is detected?
validation_requirements: Check initialization flow for maxStakingIncentive default values
Test changeIncentiveFractions without changeStakingParams and verify checkpoint behavior
Verify if calculateStakingIncentives handles zero maxStakingAmount correctly

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; staking incentives silently zeroed
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until admin corrects via changeStakingParams + next checkpoint

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All staking targets receive zero incentives despite inflation allocation

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Total loss of staking incentives for affected epochs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Default values of StakingPoint struct fields
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace staking parameter propagation across epochs

threats.title: DefaultTargetDispenserL2 processDataMaintenance Allows Owner to Bypass Staking Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: The processDataMaintenance function allows the owner to call _processData with arbitrary data, which routes through the same verification pipeline as bridge messages. However, the owner can craft data targeting any address with any amount. While _processData does check via stakingFactory, the function effectively gives the owner ability to queue deposits to arbitrary targets.
threats.investigation_rationale: processDataMaintenance exists for disaster recovery (bridge failures). However, it bypasses bridge authentication entirely - the owner supplies both targets and amounts directly. If the owner is compromised or malicious, they can direct withheld/queued OLAS to any target, even ones that fail verification (which would be withheld, not deposited). The concern is that valid-appearing targets receive unearned deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: processDataMaintenance calls _processData with owner-supplied data, bypassing bridge message authentication; targets still verified by factory but amounts are unconstrained

preconditions: L2 target dispenser owner is compromised or acts maliciously
L2 dispenser has OLAS balance (from previous bridge transfers or withheld amounts)
Owner crafts data to direct OLAS to specific staking targets

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No bridge authentication; owner provides (targets, amounts) directly
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised owner directs all OLAS balance to attacker-controlled staking contracts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full OLAS balance of L2 dispenser extracted via attacker-controlled staking targets

economic_questions: How much OLAS typically sits in L2 dispensers?
Does stakingFactory verification adequately limit the damage?
Is the owner a timelock/multisig or a single EOA?
validation_requirements: Verify that processDataMaintenance data is processed identically to bridge messages
Confirm factory verification still applies to maintenance-supplied targets
Check if amount limits are enforced on maintenance data

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very high if balance is significant

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify factory verification limits damage in maintenance path

threats.title: Permanent Tokenomics Freeze via Prolonged Checkpoint Delay
threats.hypothesis_description: If `Tokenomics.checkpoint()` is not called for a duration exceeding `MAX_EPOCH_LENGTH` (~364 days), it will permanently return `false`. Because `checkpoint()` is required to advance the epoch, the tokenomics system becomes permanently frozen, halting all reward distributions, bond programs, and inflation minting.
threats.investigation_rationale: While highly unlikely under normal operations, this represents a strict state transition failure mode. A prolonged network outage, governance failure, or extreme gas griefing could trigger this, resulting in a completely bricked protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `if (diffNumSeconds < curEpochLen || diffNumSeconds > MAX_EPOCH_LENGTH) { return false; }`

preconditions: No user or bot calls `checkpoint()` for `MAX_EPOCH_LENGTH` seconds.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hard upper bound on time between state transitions with no recovery mechanism.

operational_questions: Is there any admin override to force an epoch transition if this limit is breached?
Can the proxy implementation be upgraded to remove this check if it happens?
validation_requirements: Verify that `checkpoint` permanently fails after the time limit.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Protocol experiences extreme inactivity or attacker continuously griefs `checkpoint` transactions.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Time since last epoch end exceeds `MAX_EPOCH_LENGTH`.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Extremely high (requires preventing all users from calling a public function for a year).

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete and permanent halt of the tokenomics system.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires upgrading the Tokenomics implementation.

threats.title: Tokenomics.checkpoint() can be re-entered via Treasury.rebalanceTreasury(), potentially corrupting epoch transitions and breaking epoch monotonicity
threats.hypothesis_description: Tokenomics.checkpoint performs an external call to ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) before incrementing epochCounter. Tokenomics contains a _locked variable but does not use it in checkpoint(). If the Treasury is malicious/compromised or contains an unintended callback into Tokenomics.checkpoint(), it may cause nested checkpoint execution with inconsistent state (prevEpochTime/endTime/effectiveBond/maxBond) and potentially violate epochCounter monotonicity expectations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a high-impact integrity risk but low realism under the stated trust model (Treasury is trusted). Still, it is an operational hazard if Treasury is upgraded or replaced and accidentally becomes re-entrant.
deployment_assumptions: Treasury contract is upgradable or can be replaced via Tokenomics.changeManagers
Treasury.rebalanceTreasury can call back into Tokenomics.checkpoint (directly or indirectly)
threats.config_evidence: Tokenomics.changeManagers allows owner to update treasury; Tokenomics.checkpoint external-calls treasury without reentrancy guard.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External call ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(...) occurs before epochCounter is updated; no _locked check in checkpoint()

preconditions: Treasury.rebalanceTreasury makes a (re)entrant call into Tokenomics.checkpoint() or a path that triggers it
Epoch timing checks pass for both frames (diffNumSeconds within bounds, lastDonationBlockNumber != block.number)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reentrancy lock exists in Tokenomics but not applied to checkpoint
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires compromised/malicious treasury or unsafe upgrade

operational_questions: Does the deployed Treasury have any callback behavior into Tokenomics?
Is Treasury upgradeable and governed by the same DAO/owner?
Are there monitoring controls preventing treasury upgrades to unsafe code?
validation_requirements: Review Treasury implementation and any hooks invoked in rebalanceTreasury
Simulate reentrant Treasury in tests to see if nested checkpoint can succeed
Assess whether timestamp/diff checks prevent reentrant second settlement

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Introduce or compromise Treasury with reentrant rebalanceTreasury
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call Tokenomics.checkpoint() to trigger external call
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During rebalanceTreasury, call back into Tokenomics.checkpoint() to attempt double-settlement or state corruption
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit corrupted epoch state to misallocate incentives/effectiveBond
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires control over Treasury code path

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High: may require governance intervention and state repair/migration

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Nested checkpoint traces; unusual multiple EpochSettled events in one tx

threats.title: Unbounded call cost in Tokenomics.trackServiceDonations() (external registry queries per service/unit) enables griefing against Treasury-triggered donation tracking by choosing services with extreme unit counts
threats.hypothesis_description: trackServiceDonations loops: services -> unitTypes(2) -> units(numServiceUnits) and performs multiple external calls (getUnitIdsOfService, ownerOf for service/unit owners, ve votes checks). Although only Treasury can call trackServiceDonations, an attacker can influence which services donations are attributed to (depending on Treasury deposit workflow) and select services with very large unit lists, inflating gas and potentially causing Treasury donation-processing transactions to revert or become too costly, delaying reward accounting for an epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an ops-facing DoS: if Treasury has any permissionless donation intake that later batches tracking on-chain, an attacker can craft inputs (services with high unit counts) to exceed gas limits.
deployment_assumptions: Treasury aggregates/relays donation attribution inputs that an unprivileged attacker can influence
ServiceRegistry permits services with very large component/agent sets
Treasury uses batch processing on-chain within fixed gas limits
threats.config_evidence: No Treasury source provided; Tokenomics.trackServiceDonations explicitly trusts Treasury for validation but performs heavy loops and external calls.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Nested loops over services and units; external calls to ServiceRegistry.getUnitIdsOfService and registries.ownerOf inside loops

preconditions: Attacker can cause Treasury to call trackServiceDonations including attacker-chosen serviceIds
Chosen services have large numServiceUnits values

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Heavy per-unit external calls in a single transaction
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires Treasury intake design to be attacker-influenced

operational_questions: Can an unprivileged donor choose arbitrary serviceIds for donation attribution?
Does Treasury ever call trackServiceDonations with many services per tx?
What are realistic upper bounds on getUnitIdsOfService return sizes?
validation_requirements: Review Treasury donation intake and batching strategy
Benchmark gas vs number of services/units on a fork
Add fuzz tests with maximal unit arrays

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Donate in a way that causes Treasury to attribute donations to that service
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Force Treasury’s trackServiceDonations call to exceed gas and revert repeatedly
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Delay epoch incentive accounting and downstream claims

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: Treasury may need to split batches or introduce caps

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated reverts of Treasury donation-processing txs; high gas usage spikes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Backlog of untracked donations; mismatch between received ETH and accounted totalDonationsETH updates
